name: Anjali Sud
title: The Vimeo Plot Twist: How Anjali Sud Outsmarted the Streaming Wars
dek: Everyone expected Vimeo to fight YouTube and lose. Anjali Sud quietly changed the entire game instead, and became one of tech's most admired CEOs doing it.
body_html:
Here's a quiz: how do you win against YouTube? Trick question. You don't, and Anjali Sud figured that out faster than anyone. Instead of throwing Vimeo into a doomed cage match for eyeballs and ad dollars, she did something almost sneaky in its brilliance: she changed what Vimeo was even for. The result turned a beloved-but-niche video site into a serious software business, and turned Anjali into one of the most respected leaders in tech.

The Come-Up: From Marketing Manager to the Corner Office

Anjali didn't parachute in as a celebrity CEO. She climbed. After business school and stints in finance and e-commerce, she joined Vimeo and reportedly started in a marketing role, the kind of job that doesn't usually end with running the whole company. But she kept spotting the thing leadership was missing, and she kept being right about it. She rose through the ranks and, while still relatively young for a major tech chief, took the top job and immediately made a bet that raised eyebrows.

The Creativity: Selling Shovels in a Gold Rush

The insight was deceptively simple. Millions of businesses, creators, and teams needed to make, host, and share video, but they didn't want to become YouTube stars or fight an algorithm for scraps of attention. They needed tools. So Anjali repositioned Vimeo away from being a consumer streaming destination and toward being the software platform that powers everybody else's video, hosting, privacy controls, editing, analytics, and the unglamorous-but-essential plumbing.

While everyone else dug for gold in the streaming wars, she sold the shovels, and the picks, and the maps.

It was a masterclass in brand jiu-jitsu. Vimeo had always carried a reputation for taste, for being the place serious filmmakers and designers parked their work, a little more curated and a little less chaotic than the alternatives. Anjali took that aesthetic credibility and pointed it at a massive, underserved business market. The product world got cleaner, more self-serve, more obviously useful to a small company or a solo creator, and the whole company's center of gravity shifted from views to value.

The Payoff: From Niche Player to Public Company

The pivot worked. Vimeo's software-first model fueled real growth, and the company was eventually spun out and taken public, a milestone that put a substantial market value on the business Anjali had reinvented. She became a widely cited example of how to lead a turnaround by changing the question rather than grinding harder at the wrong answer, and landed on plenty of most-powerful and most-admired-leader lists along the way.

What makes her story resonate for a younger generation of builders is the lesson underneath it: you do not have to win the fight everyone tells you to fight. Sometimes the smartest, most creative move is to walk off that battlefield entirely and start a better game where you already hold the advantage. Anjali Sud didn't beat YouTube. She refused to play, and she won anyway.

She looked at the streaming war, said no thanks, and built an empire selling everyone else the camera.
